displayName: Watching Water Leave a Steel Plate
description: Interactive simulation based on NCERT Class 6 Chapter 8 (Activity 8.2) — students predict, observe water evaporating from a steel plate, flip the plate to check its dry underside, and complete a 5-question quiz to confirm understanding of evaporation.
subject: Science
chapter: Chapter 8 — A Journey through States of Water

1.0.1: Rewrote evaporation: stable wispy vapour particles (random props baked at creation, no per-frame jitter).
Vapour now emanates from the shrinking water pool, sways and fades as it rises.
Added a soft heat-haze band on the water surface during evaporation.
Fixed flip-plate underside label overlap — '(no water)' is hidden when the 'DRY' label is shown.

animationSpeed.description: Animation speed multiplier — higher = faster evaporation animation

autoPlayDuration.description: Auto-advance delay in ms. 0 = manual navigation only (recommended for this tool)

additionalProps.description: Tool-specific dynamic content passed by the agent to customise the simulation

showPredictStep.description: Show or hide the Predict scene (Scene 1). Hide for assessment-only mode.

showFlipStep.description: Show or hide the Flip Plate scene (Scene 3). Hide if you only need observe + quiz.

startScene.description: Which scene to open on. Defaults to 'predict' if showPredictStep is true, else 'observe'.

waterColor.description: Colour of the water pool on the plate (hex)

vapourColor.description: Colour of the rising vapour wisps and surface haze (hex)

evaporationSpeed.description: Seconds for water to fully evaporate at animationSpeed = 1. Lower = faster demo.

predict.title: Make Your Prediction
predict.description: Student selects where they think the water will go before watching the simulation. Prediction must be locked before play is enabled.
options: It will seep through to the underside
It will evaporate into the air as vapour
It will stay on the plate forever

observe.title: Observe the Simulation
observe.description: Animated steel plate with a water pool that shrinks over time. Wispy vapour rises from the pool, sways and fades. Water % counter shown. Play / Pause / Reset controls.
features: Water pool shrinks via SVG animation
Wispy vapour particles rise, drift and fade (props baked at creation — no flicker)
Vapour emanates from the shrinking pool; spawn rate scales with remaining water
Soft heat-haze band on the water surface while evaporating
Next step button appears at 55% evaporation

flip.title: Check the Underside
flip.description: CSS 3D perspective flip (rotateX 180°) reveals the completely dry underside. Student answers where the water went — immediate feedback.
features: Back face: dry underside with single DRY label (no overlapping text)

quiz.title: 5-Question Quiz
quiz.description: One question at a time with animated dot progress bar. Immediate explanation shown after each answer. Colour-coded correct/wrong feedback.

questions.topic: What do rising vapour particles represent?

results.title: Results & Concept Recap
results.description: Conic-gradient score circle, per-question correct/wrong review bubbles, key concept summary card, Try Again and Watch Again buttons.

scoreMessages.3-4: Good work! Review the key concept once more.
scoreMessages.0-2: Observe the simulation again carefully and retry!

animations: wsp-vapor — vapour wisp rises, sways and fades
wsp-haze — heat-haze band on the water surface

teachConcept.description: Full inquiry flow: predict → observe → flip → quiz

fastClassDemo.description: Skip prediction, speed up evaporation for classroom projection

quizOnly.description: Jump straight to the 5-question quiz for revision

slowObservation.description: Slow evaporation for detailed classroom observation

ncertAlignment.chapterTitle: A Journey through States of Water
ncertAlignment.keyConceptTaught: Water evaporates from a steel plate — it does not seep through metal. The dry underside is evidence. The process is evaporation.
learningObjectives: Understand that water can change state from liquid to gas (water vapour)
Recognise that water does NOT seep through a solid metal surface
Identify evaporation as the process responsible for 'disappearing' water
Use the scientific inquiry method: predict → observe → conclude
Apply the concept to real-life examples (puddles, wet clothes, mopped floors)
